fields.name: bcrypt
fields.intro_template:
## bcrypt

A password hash built on Blowfish's deliberately slow key schedule, iterated 2^cost times. The most-deployed password hash in 2025 — Rails, PHP, Spring Security defaults.

fields.title: bcrypt: Blowfish slowed down on purpose

fields.prompt_template:
## bcrypt — a password hash built on Blowfish

**bcrypt** was designed by **Niels Provos and David Mazières** in 1999 for OpenBSD's password storage. Twenty-six years later it's still the most-deployed password hash on the internet:

- **Ruby on Rails** — `has_secure_password` uses bcrypt by default.
- **PHP frameworks** — Symfony, Laravel, WordPress, Drupal all ship bcrypt as the default.
- **Spring Security** — `BCryptPasswordEncoder` was the default until very recently (now Argon2id in newer versions).
- **Node.js** — `bcrypt` and `bcryptjs` are the most-installed password-hashing packages on npm.

### Why it's slow on purpose

The whole design point of bcrypt is **deliberate slowness with a tunable cost factor**. A single hash takes ~250 ms at cost 12 (the modern default) — invisible to the user at login, but ruinous to an attacker who needs to try billions of guesses against a leaked database.

The interesting part is **how** bcrypt achieves that slowness. Rather than iterate a fast hash (the [PBKDF2 approach](/algorithms/password-hashing/learn/slow-on-purpose/) you saw in the Password Hashing lesson), bcrypt iterates the **key schedule of Blowfish** — a phase of the Blowfish cipher that's already deliberately slow because it has to scramble 4 KB of S-boxes and an 18-entry P-array starting from the hex digits of π.

### Cross-reference

If you've done the [Blowfish lesson](/algorithms/blowfish/learn/feistel-rounds/), this is the same key schedule — repurposed. That lesson mentioned in passing that Blowfish's setup is famously slow, and that bcrypt exploits this. This lesson is the deep dive.

If you've done the [Password Hashing lesson](/algorithms/password-hashing/learn/slow-on-purpose/), bcrypt was the middle row of the side-by-side comparison — covered briefly with cited timings. This lesson runs a live timing demo, walks the EksBlowfish construction, and explores the things that lesson skipped (the 72-byte limit, the `$2b$` format, when to migrate to Argon2id).

### What this lesson covers

- **Step 2** — Blowfish's key schedule and why it's the slowest part of any Blowfish encryption.
- **Step 3** — EksBlowfish ("Expensive Key Schedule Blowfish"): bcrypt's iterated variant of that schedule.
- **Step 4** — Interactive: pick a cost factor, see how wall-clock time scales (live demo + cited real-bcrypt numbers).
- **Step 5** — The `$2b$12$<salt><hash>` format, the 72-byte password limit, and the `$2a$/$2x$/$2y$` history.
- **Step 6** — bcrypt vs Argon2id vs PBKDF2: when to pick which.
- **Step 7** — Runnable Python codegen using the `bcrypt` library.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** bcrypt is a password hash built on Blowfish's already-slow setup phase, iterated 2^cost times. Cost 12 = 4096 iterations ≈ 250 ms ≈ default for most frameworks. Still the most-deployed password hash in 2025. This lesson is the deep dive on the construction, the timing, and the operational footguns.

fields.prompt_template:
### Blowfish's already-slow key schedule

Most block ciphers' key schedules are cheap. AES-128 expands a 16-byte key into 11 round keys in about 11 AES round-equivalents — call it a few microseconds. The same is true for ChaCha20, 3DES, and most things you'd reach for.

**Blowfish is the exception.** Its key schedule is *extravagantly* expensive. Schneier wrote it that way on purpose — to push some of the cost of a brute-force attack into the setup phase, where an attacker testing many candidate keys has to pay it once per guess.

### The procedure

Blowfish's key schedule sets up two pieces of state: an **18-entry P-array** (each 32 bits) and **four S-boxes** (each 256 entries × 32 bits = 1 KB; 4 KB total). The setup runs:

1. **Initialize from π.** P-array and S-boxes start as the hex digits of π — fixed constants, the same for every Blowfish setup. (`P[0] = 0x243f6a88`, the first 32 bits of π's fractional part in hex.)
2. **XOR the user key into the P-array.** The key (1-56 bytes) is cycled to fill all 18 P-entries — `P[0] ^= key[0..3]`, `P[1] ^= key[4..7]`, wrapping around if the key is shorter.
3. **Encrypt the all-zero block under the current state.** Blowfish-encrypt the 64-bit value `0x0000000000000000` using P and the S-boxes as currently set. Store the resulting 64-bit ciphertext as the *new* `P[0]` and `P[1]`.
4. **Repeat.** Blowfish-encrypt the just-produced output (using the further-updated state), store as `P[2]` and `P[3]`. Then again for `P[4]` and `P[5]`. Continue until all 18 P-entries are replaced.
5. **Keep going through the S-boxes.** Same procedure replaces every entry of S1, S2, S3, S4 — that's 4 × 256 / 2 = **512** more Blowfish-encrypt calls.

### The arithmetic

- 9 encryptions to replace the 18 P-entries (2 per encryption).
- 512 encryptions to replace the 1024 S-box entries (2 per encryption).
- **Total: ~521 full Blowfish encryptions per key setup.**

For comparison: encrypting one 64-bit block of plaintext is 1 Blowfish encryption. So **changing the key in Blowfish costs roughly 521× as much as encrypting one block**. That's wildly inefficient for a cipher you're going to use to bulk-encrypt a long stream — but it's exactly the property bcrypt wants.

### Schneier's design note

Schneier wrote in [*Applied Cryptography*](https://www.schneier.com/books/applied-cryptography/) that this slow setup was intentional: "This makes the cipher unsuitable for applications that require fast key changes, such as packet switching. But it makes brute-force attacks slower."

bcrypt takes that already-slow setup and runs it **2^cost** more times. The next step covers how.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** Blowfish's key schedule is famously slow — running it once costs about as much as encrypting 521 blocks. The setup XORs your key into the P-array, then re-encrypts the all-zero block repeatedly to replace P and the S-boxes one chunk at a time. Most ciphers' key schedules are 1-2 block-encryptions. Blowfish's is 521. bcrypt picks this up and iterates it.

fields.prompt_template:
### EksBlowfish — Expensive Key Schedule Blowfish

bcrypt's internal cipher is a slight modification of Blowfish that Provos and Mazières called **EksBlowfish**. The encryption step is exactly the same as standard Blowfish. The **key schedule** is what differs — and that's where all the cost lives.

### The construction

Standard Blowfish's key schedule takes a single argument: the user key. EksBlowfish's takes **three**: a salt, a password, and a cost factor.

```
EksBlowfishSetup(salt, password, cost):
    state := InitialState  // P-array + S-boxes from π's hex digits
    state := ExpandKey(state, salt, password)  // mix in BOTH salt and password
    for i in 1 .. 2^cost:
        state := ExpandKey(state, 0,    password)  // re-mix password under zero salt
        state := ExpandKey(state, 0,    salt)      // re-mix salt under zero salt
    return state
```

The `ExpandKey(state, salt, key)` primitive is a small generalization of the standard Blowfish key schedule (step 2). When `salt = 0` it's identical. When `salt ≠ 0`, the salt bytes get XORed into the data being Blowfish-encrypted at each step, in addition to the key bytes XORed into the P-array. This binds the salt into every round of the setup.

### What the cost factor controls

`cost` is the **log₂** of the iteration count. The body of the loop runs `2^cost` times:

| cost | Iterations | Approximate work |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 16 | ~16 × (521 + 521) Blowfish encryptions = ~16K |
| 8 | 256 | ~256 × ~1000 = ~256K |
| 10 | 1,024 | ~1M Blowfish encryptions |
| 12 | 4,096 | ~4M Blowfish encryptions |
| 14 | 16,384 | ~16M Blowfish encryptions |
| 16 | 65,536 | ~65M Blowfish encryptions |

Each `+1` to the cost **doubles** the total work. That's the exponential cost knob — and why the same `+1` can mean either "unnoticeable" or "unusable" depending on which end of the curve you're on.

### Producing the actual hash output

Once the loop finishes, EksBlowfish has a fully scrambled state (P-array + S-boxes) that's specific to this (salt, password) pair. To produce the hash:

```
ciphertext := "OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt"  // 24-byte ASCII constant
for i in 1 .. 64:
    ciphertext := EksBlowfish-ECB-encrypt(state, ciphertext)
return ciphertext  // 24 bytes
```

**"OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt"** is an arbitrary 24-byte ASCII constant baked into the spec — every bcrypt hash on Earth encrypts this same string. The output of 64 rounds of ECB encryption under the EksBlowfish state is the **24-byte raw hash**, which gets base64-encoded into the 31-character hash suffix in the final `$2b$...` string.

### Why 64 ECB encryptions of a constant?

It's a deterministic stretching step that turns the EksBlowfish *state* (which is large — ~4 KB of S-boxes + 72 bytes of P-array) into a *fixed-size* 24-byte output that can be displayed and stored. The 64-encryption loop is overkill cryptographically (the state is already thoroughly randomized by the 2^cost-iteration setup), but it's cheap relative to the setup phase, so the spec just locks it in.

The upshot: when you call `bcrypt.hashpw("password", bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))`, the library is doing 2 × 2^12 = 8,192 ExpandKey calls (about 8 million Blowfish encryptions of all-zero blocks, plus the salt and password mixing) followed by 64 EksBlowfish ECB encryptions of "OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt". The next step lets you feel the cost on your own machine.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** bcrypt's setup is the Blowfish key schedule, run 2^cost times in a loop that alternates re-mixing the password and re-mixing the salt. Cost 12 = 4,096 loops ≈ 8 million Blowfish encryptions ≈ ~250 ms. Then it encrypts the literal ASCII string "OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt" 64 times to squeeze the giant EksBlowfish state into a 24-byte output. That 24-byte output, base64-encoded, is the hash you see at the end of the `$2b$...` string.

fields.prompt_template:
### Feel the cost factor

Pick a cost factor and a short password. The widget runs a **synthetic** bcrypt-style timing demo: PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 with `iterations = 1000 × 2^cost`. The shape of the curve is right — `cost +1` doubles the work, same as real bcrypt — but the constant factor is different (PBKDF2 of SHA-256 is cheaper per iteration than a Blowfish key-schedule pass). To make the absolute numbers honest, we also display the **cited real-bcrypt timing** for the same cost factor from `bcrypt` (Python, C-backed) on a typical 2024 laptop.

### Why synthetic?

A faithful JavaScript bcrypt (e.g. `bcryptjs`) is ~30 KB of code, would block the main thread for hundreds of ms per hash at cost 12, and is overkill for a step whose pedagogical point is **feeling the exponential curve**. PBKDF2 is built into Web Crypto, so this widget runs zero extra bytes. Step 7's codegen uses the real `bcrypt` Python library.

### Cited real-bcrypt timings (2024 laptop)

| Cost | Iterations (2^cost) | Real bcrypt time |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 16 | ~1 ms |
| 8 | 256 | ~16 ms |
| 10 | 1,024 | ~65 ms |
| 12 | 4,096 | ~260 ms |
| 14 | 16,384 | ~1.0 s |

### What to look for

- Pick cost 4. Note the ms.
- Pick cost 8. Should be roughly **16×** the cost-4 time (2⁸/2⁴ = 16).
- Pick cost 12. Should be **~16×** the cost-8 time again — and ~256× the cost-4 time.
- Pick cost 14. Should be **~4×** the cost-12 time. Around a second.

That's the exponential cost knob. **Cost 12 is the modern default** — invisible at login, ~260 ms on a 2024 laptop. Cost 14 is for security-conscious deployments that can afford ~1 s per login. Cost 16+ exists, but the latency starts becoming user-visible.

Attackers see the same multiplier going the other way. A leaked DB of cost-12 hashes takes 256× longer to brute-force than a leaked DB of cost-4 hashes. That's the whole point.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:** Pick a cost. Submit. Watch the ms. Pick a higher cost. Submit again — should be ~2^(diff) times slower. The widget uses PBKDF2 as a stand-in for bcrypt (real bcrypt in JS is heavy), so the ms numbers are synthetic — but the curve is right. The cited column shows what real bcrypt takes for the same cost on a 2024 laptop.

**Practical:** Cost 12 ≈ 260 ms ≈ default for most frameworks. Each +1 doubles the cost. Pick the highest your login latency budget can absorb.

fields.prompt_template:
### The `$2b$` format and why it's everything-in-one-string

A real bcrypt hash looks like this:

```
$2b$12$N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMyeIjZAgcfl7p92ldGxad68LJZdL17lhWy
```

60 characters total, encoded so it's safe in any database, URL, config file, or HTTP header. Decomposed:

| Bytes | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `$2b$` | **Algorithm version tag.** 2b = current. (See history below.) |
| `12$` | **Cost factor.** 2^12 = 4,096 iterations of the EksBlowfish key schedule. |
| `N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMye` | **22-character base64 salt.** This is 16 random bytes encoded in bcrypt's variant of base64 (different from standard base64 — the alphabet is `./A-Za-z0-9` and there's no padding). |
| `IjZAgcfl7p92ldGxad68LJZdL17lhWy` | **31-character base64 hash.** This is the 24-byte raw EksBlowfish output (the "OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt" encryption from step 3), base64-encoded. |

### Why everything-in-one-string matters

- **One DB column.** You don't need a separate `salt` column, or a `cost` column, or an `algorithm_version` column. One `VARCHAR(60)` and you're done.
- **Self-verifying.** `bcrypt.checkpw(password, stored_hash)` parses the cost and salt back out of the stored string. No matter what cost or salt was used at signup, verification just works.
- **Self-upgrading.** Read the cost prefix at login time. If it's lower than your current default, re-hash at the new cost and update the DB. The user never notices. Many production systems do this — it's how you smoothly migrate from cost 10 to cost 12 to cost 14 as hardware speeds up.

Argon2 uses the same idea with a richer prefix (`$argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$...`) — both formats are part of the broader [PHC string format](https://github.com/P-H-C/phc-string-format).

### The 72-byte password limit (a real footgun)

bcrypt **silently truncates passwords to 72 bytes**. Anything longer hashes identically to its 72-byte prefix:

```python
import bcrypt
long = b"A" * 100
salt = bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=4)
h1 = bcrypt.hashpw(long, salt)
h2 = bcrypt.hashpw(b"A" * 72, salt)  # same 72-byte prefix
assert h1 == h2  # ← they collide!
```

This has caused real CVEs:

- **CVE-2024-9180 (Django)** — Django's `BCryptSHA256PasswordHasher` pre-hashes with SHA-256 to dodge the 72-byte cap. Code that bypassed the hasher's `harden_runtime` path could be tricked into truncating. Patched in Django 5.0.10.
- **Bitwarden incident (2024)** — Master passwords longer than 72 bytes silently shared the same hash, defeating the security benefit of long passphrases. Fixed by pre-hashing with SHA-256.

**Mitigations:**

- **Pre-hash long inputs.** `bcrypt.hashpw(sha256(password).hexdigest()[:72], salt)` — common pattern in Django and others. SHA-256 of any input is 64 hex chars = 64 bytes, safely under 72.
- **Pick Argon2id for new systems.** Argon2 has no length cap.
- **Document the limit** if you must use bcrypt and accept passphrases.

### History of the version tags

bcrypt has had several version prefixes; the rest exist for compatibility reasons only.

| Tag | Status | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| `$2$` | Obsolete | Original 1999 spec. Don't see it in modern code. |
| `$2a$` | Legacy | The first widely-deployed version. Has a known null-byte bug — embedded `\0` in passwords could truncate hash input. |
| `$2x$` | Emergency patch | Marker for PHP's buggy `crypt_blowfish` from 2011. Should never appear in new hashes. |
| `$2y$` | Emergency patch | PHP's fix for the `$2x$` bug. Functionally identical to `$2a$` with the null-byte fix. |
| `$2b$` | **Current** | OpenBSD's corrected version. Use this for everything new. |

The `bcrypt` Python library writes `$2b$` by default. `bcryptjs` writes `$2a$` historically — both verify all variants, so legacy hashes don't need to be re-derived just for the version tag. Just check the prefix at login: if it's anything other than `$2b$`, mark for upgrade at next verify-success.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:** A bcrypt hash is 60 characters: `$2b$<cost>$<22 salt chars><31 hash chars>`. Everything (algorithm, cost, salt, hash) travels in one string. One DB column. Verify just works.

**Watch out:** bcrypt silently truncates passwords longer than 72 bytes — caused real CVEs at Django and Bitwarden. Pre-hash with SHA-256 if you accept passphrases, or pick Argon2id.

**Version tags:** `$2b$` is current. Anything else (`$2a$`, `$2x$`, `$2y$`) is legacy or buggy-PHP-era — mark for upgrade on next login.

fields.prompt_template:
### bcrypt vs Argon2id vs PBKDF2 — when to pick which

You now know bcrypt deeply. The [Password Hashing lesson](/algorithms/password-hashing/learn/slow-on-purpose/) compared all three at a high level. This step is the **operational** decision tree.

### Side-by-side

| | bcrypt | Argon2id | PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Released** | 1999 | 2015 (PHC winner) | 1999 (RFC 8018) |
| **Cost knob** | Single `cost` (logarithmic, 2^c) | Memory (`m`), time (`t`), parallelism (`p`) | Single `iter` (linear) |
| **Memory-hard?** | No (~4 KB state) | **Yes** (tunable, default 64 MB) | No |
| **Password length** | 72 bytes (truncates silently) | No limit | No limit |
| **Output format** | `$2b$<c>$<salt><hash>` (60 chars) | `$argon2id$v=19$m=...$<salt>$<hash>` | Raw bytes (no standard format) |
| **FIPS-approved?** | No | No | **Yes** (SP 800-132) |
| **Stdlib in Python** | No (`pip install bcrypt`) | No (`pip install argon2-cffi`) | **Yes** (`hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac`) |
| **Stdlib in Java/Node/Go** | Standard | Standard | Standard |
| **2024 default** | Many existing systems | New systems | FIPS-required systems |

### The decision matrix

| Situation | Pick | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **New greenfield system, no constraints** | **Argon2id** | Memory-hardness makes GPU/ASIC attacks dramatically more expensive. RFC 9106 default. |
| **Already running bcrypt** | **Stay on bcrypt at cost 12+** | Migration cost is real (re-hash at next login + grace period). bcrypt is fine. |
| **FIPS / FedRAMP / regulated** | **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256** at iter ≥ 600,000 | The other two aren't FIPS-approved. OWASP recommends 600K+ in 2023. |
| **Embedded device, RAM-constrained** | Argon2id with low-memory profile | RFC 9106 publishes `m=19MB, t=2, p=1` as the low-memory recommended profile. Or bcrypt cost 10-11 if even 19 MB is too much. |
| **Need a single-file zero-dep solution** | bcrypt | Smaller dependency footprint than `argon2-cffi`. Mature, no surprises. |
| **Migrating from MD5/SHA1/raw SHA-256** | Argon2id over re-hashed legacy | Layer: `Argon2id(legacy_hash, salt)` at storage, re-derive cleanly on next login, mark for upgrade. |
| **Worried about GPU/ASIC farms specifically** | **Argon2id**, no contest | bcrypt's ~4 KB state lets a GPU run thousands in parallel. Argon2id's 64 MB per hash forces RAM, not parallelism. |

### The case for sticking with bcrypt

bcrypt has been deployed continuously since 1999 with **zero known practical attacks**. That's 26 years of cryptanalysis. Argon2 is younger (10 years) and although it has solid academic backing — PHC winner, RFC 9106 — "newer" carries some risk all on its own. If you have a bcrypt deployment and aren't suffering from GPU-cracking attacks (most deployments aren't), the migration cost may exceed the security gain.

The consensus recommendation from OWASP, NIST, and most security-conscious shops in 2024:

- **New code → Argon2id.**
- **Existing bcrypt → keep bcrypt, just bump the cost factor over time.**
- **FIPS-required → PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 at iter ≥ 600,000.**

### What about scrypt?

[scrypt](https://www.tarsnap.com/scrypt.html) (Percival, 2009) was the **first widely-deployed memory-hard password hash**. Argon2id is its conceptual descendant and won the PHC bake-off explicitly designed to replace it. If you're on scrypt at sane parameters, you're fine — there's no urgent reason to migrate. If you're picking now, pick Argon2id.

### Operational reminders (apply to all three)

- **Bump cost over time.** Re-hash at login if your default has gone up. The OWASP PBKDF2 recommendation grew 60× between 2017 and 2023; bcrypt's modern default crept from 10 in 2010 to 12 in 2024.
- **Use constant-time comparison.** `bcrypt.checkpw`, `ph.verify`, and `hmac.compare_digest` all do this internally. Don't do `==` on raw hashes.
- **Rate-limit login attempts.** Password hashing slows offline attackers; rate limiting slows online ones. Both, not either.
- **Add a pepper if your threat model includes DB-only leaks.** A site-wide secret XOR'd or concatenated with the password before hashing, stored *outside* the database. Out of scope here.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:**

- **New system?** Argon2id, RFC 9106 defaults (`m=64MB, t=3, p=4`).
- **Already on bcrypt?** Stay on bcrypt, cost 12+.
- **FIPS?** PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256, iter ≥ 600,000.

bcrypt's 25-year track record is genuinely valuable — if your bcrypt deployment isn't being actively attacked, you don't need to migrate. The big gap is **memory-hardness**: Argon2id forces GPU attackers to allocate 64 MB per concurrent guess, which is the main reason it's the recommended default for greenfield systems.

fields.prompt_template:
## Done!

You've walked bcrypt end to end — from the Blowfish key schedule it rests on, through the EksBlowfish iteration construction, the `$2b$` output format, the 72-byte footgun, and the modern bcrypt-vs-Argon2id decision.

### Recap

| Step | Key idea |
|---|---|
| 1 (intro) | bcrypt is a 1999 password hash by Provos & Mazières. Still the most-deployed in 2025. |
| 2 (Blowfish connection) | Blowfish's key schedule costs ~521 block-encryptions — 521× more than encrypting one block. Deliberately slow by design. |
| 3 (EksBlowfish) | bcrypt iterates that already-slow schedule **2^cost** times. cost 12 = 4,096 iterations ≈ 8M Blowfish encryptions ≈ ~250 ms. |
| 4 (time the cost) | Each `+1` to cost doubles the work. cost 4 → cost 14 is a ~1024× slowdown. |
| 5 (salt and format) | `$2b$<cost>$<22 salt chars><31 hash chars>` — everything in one 60-char string. 72-byte password truncation is the main footgun. |
| 6 (bcrypt vs Argon2) | bcrypt for existing deployments, Argon2id for new systems, PBKDF2 for FIPS. bcrypt's 26-year track record is genuinely valuable. |

### The codegen

Click **Show full script** below. The Python uses the real `bcrypt` library (C-backed — same library Django and most Python web frameworks use under the hood) to:

1. **Hash** the demo password at your chosen cost factor.
2. **Show that the same password + same cost = two DIFFERENT hashes** (because the salt is fresh every time).
3. **Verify both hashes** against the original password (works — the salt is embedded in the hash string).
4. **Reject the wrong password** (`bcrypt.checkpw` returns `False`, no exception).
5. **Plot the cost curve** by hashing at costs 4, 8, 10, 12 and printing the wall time.

```bash
pip install bcrypt
python bcrypt_demo.py
```

### Forward links

- **[Password Hashing](/algorithms/password-hashing/learn/slow-on-purpose/)** — the high-level comparison lesson. You've now done the bcrypt deep dive that lesson skipped over with cited timings.
- **[Blowfish](/algorithms/blowfish/learn/feistel-rounds/)** — the cipher whose key schedule bcrypt iterates. Worth revisiting now that you've seen *why* the slow key schedule matters.
- **[HMAC](/algorithms/hmac/learn/mac-the-message/)** — PBKDF2's inner primitive. Different threat model, different construction.

### Things this lesson left unexplored

- **The full base64 alphabet** — bcrypt's base64 isn't standard base64. The alphabet is `./A-Za-z0-9` and there's no `=` padding. If you write a bcrypt parser by hand, you'll trip on this.
- **The `BF_ROUNDS` constant** in OpenBSD's reference implementation — it's a literal `64` for the OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt loop, hard-coded.
- **JOSE / JWT bcrypt** — bcrypt doesn't appear in the JWT family (which uses HS256, RS256, ES256, etc.). The PHC string format is its native habitat.
- **The 1999 paper** — Provos & Mazières, *"A Future-Adaptable Password Scheme"*, USENIX 1999. Short and readable; worth the 12 pages if you want the original argument for adaptability.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** bcrypt = Blowfish's already-slow key schedule, iterated 2^cost more times. cost 12 ≈ 8M Blowfish-encrypts ≈ 250 ms. Output is one self-contained string. Verify is symmetric. The 72-byte limit is the main footgun. Use the codegen below to run real bcrypt on your machine.
